truth.papers.A10.title: Extreme high accuracy prediction and design of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel using machine learning
truth.papers.A10.paper_scope: Develop a data-driven model combining machine learning, firefly optimization algorithm and conditional generative adversarial networks to predict solid solution strengthening theory of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel, and validate with three cladding samples.

prediction.papers.A10.title: Extreme high accuracy prediction and design of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel using machine learning
prediction.papers.A10.paper_scope: A machine learning-based approach combining SVR, CGANs and firefly optimization algorithm to predict and inverse-design the microhardness of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel, validated by arc cladding experiments.

truth.processes.proc_cladding.description: Arc cladding on ASTM A36 steel substrate (300×120×40 mm) using IDEALARC DC-1000; parameters: current 440-600 A, voltage 30-32 V, cladding speed 24 m/h, interlayer temperature 380-410 °C, wire feed speed 76.8 m/h.

prediction.processes.proc_cladding.description: Wire-fed arc cladding on ASTM A36 steel substrate; current 440-600 A, voltage 30-32 V, cladding speed 24 m/h, interlayer temperature 380-410 °C, wire feed speed 76.8 m/h

truth.processing_steps.sample_1.processing_ste: Current 440-600 A, voltage 30-32 V, cladding speed 24 m/h, wire feed speed 76.8 m/h. Substrate ASTM A36 steel. Coating as-deposited.

truth.structures.sample_1.overall_structure: Single-phase Fe-Cr solid solution with FCC lattice (as stated, peaks 100,200,211). Cr atoms randomly substitute Fe.
truth.structures.sample_1.microstructure_list[: Diffraction peak shift to higher 2θ due to atomic radius differences between Cr and Fe.
truth.structures.sample_2.microstructure_list[: Diffraction peak shift attributed to atomic radius differences between Cr, Ni and Fe.
truth.structures.sample_3.microstructure_list[: Diffraction peak shift due to atomic radius differences between Cr, Ni and Fe.

prediction.structures.sample_1.microstructure_list[: Diffraction peaks shifted to higher 2θ relative to standard Fe due to atomic radius differences between Cr, Ni and Fe
prediction.structures.sample_2.microstructure_list[: Diffraction peaks shifted to higher 2θ relative to standard Fe due to atomic radius differences between Cr, Ni and Fe
prediction.structures.sample_3.microstructure_list[: Diffraction peaks shifted to higher 2θ relative to standard Fe due to atomic radius differences between Cr, Ni and Fe

truth.computational_details.idx_0.simulation_m: Machine learning pipeline: data preprocessing (Z-score, isolation forest), augmentation with conditional generative adversarial networks (CGANs), feature engineering (RFE voting, Pearson correlation, exhaustive screening), regression with SVR (and other 9 models), hyperparameter optimization via Bayesian optimization and 10-fold CV, modification with solid solution strengthening theory, inverse design with FA, PSO, SA, GWO.
truth.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Three cladding samples validated with minimum error 1.17% between predicted and experimental microhardness.
truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Z=0.0056, M=3.06 (Taylor factor for FCC/BCC), G (shear modulus), δ_Ec (difference in cohesive energy), D_Ec (local cohesive energy mismatch)
Δσ_sss = Z·M·G·δ_Ec·D_Ec; the three features (G, δ_Ec, D_Ec) were selected from exhaustive screening as optimal.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.simulation_m: Support vector regression (SVR) with Bayesian hyperparameter optimization, ten-fold cross-validation, CGANs synthetic data augmentation, and firefly optimization algorithm (FA) inverse design
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Minimum prediction error of 1.17 % between the SVR-based model and experimental microhardness on three arc-cladded Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si alloys
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Z = 0.0056; M = 3.06; shear modulus G; difference in cohesive energy δ_Ec; local cohesive energy mismatch D_Ec
Δσ_sss = Z·M·G·δ_Ec·D_Ec; coupling this equation with SVR improved R² from 0.85 to 0.89 and reduced RMSE from 0.39 to 0.31

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The initial dataset from MatWeb contained 173 data points; after outlier removal and CGAN augmentation, expanded to 192 points.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Among 10 ML models, SVR achieved R²=0.85, RMSE=0.39; after incorporating modified solid solution strengthening, R² improved to 0.89, RMSE reduced to 0.31.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Feature engineering revealed that shear modulus G, difference in cohesive energy δ_Ec, and local cohesive energy mismatch D_Ec are the key features for microhardness.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Cr, Ni, Mo were identified as elements with positive impact on microhardness, while Mn, V, W showed weaker correlation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Inverse design using FA achieved a predicted maximum microhardness of 733.59 HV.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: The δ_r and ΔH_mix values for the three samples fall within the ranges reported for solid solution phase formation (δ_r < 0.066 and -11.6 < ΔH_mix < 3.2 kJ/mol).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: CGAN generator and discriminator architectures detailed in Table 1.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Hardness test conditions: load 1 kg, dwell time 20 s.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: XRD patterns indicate lattice distortion due to atomic radius differences, causing peak shifts.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Dataset construction: 173 Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel data points were initially collected from MatWeb; after Z-score and isolation forest outlier removal, 98 data points remained; CGANs with double hidden layers expanded the dataset to 192 data points.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Feature engineering: 41 features were collected and standardized; recursive feature elimination (RFE) wrapped with RF, LightGBM and XGBoost, combined with Pearson correlation screening (|PCC| ≥ 0.95), reduced candidates to 9; exhaustive screening identified the optimal 3-feature combination {δ_Ec, D_Ec, G} with the lowest cross-validation RMSE of 0.257.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: ML model comparison before solid-solution modification: SVR (R² = 0.85, RMSE = 0.39), ET (0.80, 0.45), MLP (0.81, 0.44), Ridge (0.82, 0.43), LightGBM (0.63, 0.62), XGBoost (0.79, 0.46), RF (0.74, 0.52), LR (0.76, 0.48), DT (0.72, 0.54).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Inverse design optimization predicted maximum microhardness: FA 733.59 HV, PSO 730.04 HV, GWO 708.60 HV, SA 700.37 HV; FA was selected for the highest predicted hardness.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Experimental validation errors: Sample 1 predicted 521 HV versus experimental 515 HV (1.17 % error); Sample 2 predicted 490 HV versus 500 HV (2.00 %); Sample 3 predicted 415 HV versus 424 HV (2.09 %); the minimum error of 1.17 % is lower than previously reported 2.01 % (Ren et al.) and 2.96 % (Huang et al.) for high-entropy alloy hardness prediction.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Elemental impact on microhardness from data correlation: Cr exhibited the strongest positive correlation with microhardness, followed by Ni and Mo; Mn, V and W showed weaker correlations.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Solid solution formation criteria for the experimental alloys: δ_r ranged 0.0335–0.0595 (below the 0.066 threshold) and ΔH_mix ranged -1.213 to -0.451 kJ/mol (within the -11.6 to 3.2 kJ/mol range), consistent with complete solid solution formation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: XRD detail note: Fig. 8 caption/table labels Sample 1 peaks as (1 1 0) at ~45°, (2 0 0) at ~65°, (2 1 1) at ~82°, whereas the body text describes Sample 1 as Fe-Cr with diffraction peaks 100, 200, 211 in an FCC lattice; Samples 2 and 3 are described as Fe-Cr-Ni phase.
